target.name: Cognition
target.founder: Scott Wu, Steven Hao, Walden Yan

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $1.0B total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 5 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in AI & Software. Range: $3.2B - $39.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in AI & Compute

private.name: Figure AI
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series C), post-money $39B

private.name: Skild AI
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series C), post-money $14B+

data.narrative:
Cognition is a Series C AI & Software company founded by Scott Wu, Steven Hao, and Walden Yan that has raised $1.5B+. The valuation analysis draws on a peer universe of 9 private and 1 public comparable (Palantir, PLTR), with direct stage peers including Figure AI, Skild AI, Wayve, Physical Intelligence, and Black Forest Labs.

Two triangulation methods apply to this stage and sector. Stage-progression analysis, anchoring to the $1.0B total-raised proxy and applying a typical 1.7x Series C step-up multiplier, implies a valuation of $1.7B. Peer-median analysis across five same-sector, same/adjacent-stage comparables yields a median valuation of $8.6B, with a range spanning $3.2B (Black Forest Labs, Series B) to $39.0B (Figure AI, Series C). The dispersion reflects material variance in stage timing and business trajectory within the AI frontier-tech cohort.

Triangulating these two methods produces a central estimate of $5.2B, with a low of $1.4B and a high of $9.9B. The wide range reflects substantive uncertainty around whether Cognition's business performance and market position align with lower-stage or high-outlier peer multiples. Key variables include revenue traction, user adoption, and competitive differentiation relative to the peer set—factors not captured in raised capital or stage alone. Stage-progression alone substantially undervalues relative to peer median, suggesting the peer set may reflect market expectations of sustained capital intensity and high growth in this sector.
